metadata.name: 6.1.a Capacity of small water retention facilities
metadata.priority: Increase in disposable water resources, improvement of its ecological state and chemical quality
metadata.definition: Volume of small water retention facilities.
metadata.methodology: Small water retention aims at reduction of the outflow of surface waters, with a balance of the natural environment by ensuring the protection and restoration of water resources. It is the ability to collect, detent and drain water in a particular place and time on the surface or in the soil, groundwater, vegetation and forest bad. Facilities of small retention of water are buildings and equipment for keeping most of the water in its surface and by-surface circulation. They are: - artificial water reservoirs with a capacity of 5 million cubic meters , - fish ponds , - self-weirs and water intake, - other weirs or transporting water.

metadata.name: 6.1.b Percentage of inland and coastal bathing waters with excellent water quality
metadata.priority: Increase in disposable water resources, improvement of its ecological state and chemical quality
metadata.definition: The proportion of inland and coastal bathing waters classified as having excellent water quality in relation to the total number of inland and coastal bathing waters.
metadata.methodology: The indicator enables the monitoring of long-term changes in the quality of bathing waters and does not refer to the current water quality at bathing sites.The numerator includes all inland and coastal bathing waters classified as having excellent water quality, while the denominator includes all inland and coastal bathing waters operating during the reference bathing season.The data required for the calculation of the indicator are collected by Member States of the European Union (EU) in accordance with Directive 2006/7/EC. The European Environment Agency is responsible for the compilation and dissemination of the data. In Poland, the classification of bathing water quality is carried out annually by the authorities of the State Sanitary Inspection following the bathing season, on the basis of the results of microbiological parameter testing (Escherichia coli and enterococci) conducted in accordance with monitoring schedules covering the previous four years.Based on the assessment of bathing water quality over a four-year period, the following water quality classes are distinguished: 4 Excellent – highest water quality; 3 Good – generally good water quality; 2 Sufficient – water meets the minimum quality standard; 1 Poor – water does not meet the minimum quality standard; 0 Not classified – classification is not possible due to the absence of a complete set of water quality data. The most common reasons for the absence of a water quality classification (“Not classified”) concern bathing waters that are: a) newly identified, for which data are still being collected; b) not designated by the gmina council during a given bathing season (within the four-year assessment period), resulting in interruptions in the microbiological monitoring required for the classification of bathing water quality.A “not classified” status does not automatically imply that the bathing water fails to meet the required standards. It only indicates that the period required to assign the bathing water to one of the four available quality classes has not yet elapsed.

metadata.name: 6.1.c Percentage of uniform surface water bodies with good status
metadata.priority: Increase in disposable water resources, improvement of its ecological state and chemical quality
metadata.definition: Share of surface water bodies (SWBs) of rivers, dam reservoirs and lakes classified as being in Good Status, in the total number of SWBs in the respective category.
metadata.methodology: A surface water body (SWB) is a discrete and significant element of surface waters such as a lake, reservoir, stream, river or canal, a section of a stream, river or canal, transitional waters, coastal water bodies, as well as dam reservoirs.Dam reservoirs are formed by the impoundment of river water; therefore, after the completion of their intended functions, efforts should be made to restore their natural state, which is a river. Moreover, they are not entirely artificial reservoirs, unlike, for example, excavations. Consequently, dam reservoirs are classified as heavily modified river surface water bodies.The status of river and lake surface water bodies is assessed as good or poor by analysing the results of classification of their ecological status or ecological potential (based on the results of measurements of water quality indicators comprising physicochemical, biological and hydromorphological elements). Ecological status is determined for natural surface water bodies, whereas ecological potential is determined for waters that are artificial or heavily modified due to human activity.Information on the status of surface waters (rivers, lakes, transitional and coastal waters) is obtained within the framework of water quality monitoring, which is a subsystem of the State Environmental Monitoring system. Water quality monitoring is carried out by the Chief Inspectorate of Environmental Protection on the basis of designated surface water bodies, which constitute the basic water management unit.
metadata.availability: Annual data; for the period 2015–2019, and for 2021 and 2024

metadata.name: 6.2.a Percentage of population connected to wastewater treatment plants
metadata.priority: Creation of legal and financial mechanism conducive to rational use of water and implementation of water efficiency technologies as well as development and modernization of wastewater treatment plants
metadata.definition: The share of population connected to wastewater treatment plants in total population.
metadata.methodology: Data on urban and rural population connected to wastewater treatment plants are presented on a basis of a number of people served by wastewater treatment plants operating within a sewage network. Estimation of population served by wastewater treatment plants is done on the basis of contracts on sewerage, number of connections to sewage network, data on people using wastewater treatment plants gathered from housing cooperatives or data from planned wastewater treatment plants. Estimated number of population served by wastewater treatment plants cannot be higher than the number of people living in a given place (as of 20 June of reporting period). Estimated number of population served by wastewater treatment plants does not include people temporary staying in a given place (e.g. tourists).

metadata.name: 6.2.b Proportion of wastewater safely treated
metadata.priority: Creation of legal and financial mechanism conducive to rational use of water and implementation of water efficiency technologies as well as development and modernization of wastewater treatment plants
metadata.definition: The ratio of the quantity of industrial and municipal waste water treated biologically, chemically and with an increased removal of nutrients, to the quantity of waste requiring purification discharged waters or into the ground within a year.
metadata.methodology: Industrial wastewater includes sewage which is not domestic wastewater or rainwater produced as a result of commercial, industrial, storage, transportation or service activity as well as water mixed with sewage produced by other entities discharged by sewage network owned by this plant. The types and amount of pollutants in industrial wastewater are very diverse. They depend on the type of industry, type of raw materials used in production and the production technology used. In statistics, the industrial wastewater also include cooling water, polluted and saline waters from mine drainage and sewage works as well as social and living wastewaters related to manufacturing processes.  Data on industrial wastewater refer to sewage discharged by the entities, which according to Polish Classification of Activity were included under 'Industry' covering ”Mining and quarrying”, ”Manufacturing”, ”Electricity, gas, steam and air conditioning supply” and “Water supply sewerage, waste management and remediation activities” as well as other sections, whose share in the volume of discharged sewage is insignificant. Industrial wastewater requiring treatment means sewage discharged via a network of open canals or ditches directly to waters, ground or sewage network from production entities (including water contaminated as a result of dehydration of mines and contaminated waters used in industry for cooling). Municipal wastewater means domestic wastewater or mixture of domestic wastewater with industrial sewage and rainwater disposed of by a commune through sewage water system and wastewater treatment plants.Data on municipal wastewaters cover sewage discharged via a sewage network by the units managed by water supply and sewage companies and plants established by a voivode (or managed by territorial self-local governments) and from 1994 all units supervising collective discharge of sewage via sewage network (including housing cooperatives, water companies, water service plants, enterprises etc.) Prior to discharge to the recipient, all the sewage should be treated. Thus, in the statistics, the wastewater means wastewater requiring treatment. The data do not include precipitation and infiltration water discharged via sewage network. Wastewater treated is wastewater subjected to the process of adjusting to environmental standards or other quality standards. Three methods are distinguished: mechanical, chemical and biological processes or increased biogene removal (including chemical). For the purpose of calculating the total amount of treated wastewater, only the amount which was subjected to the highest mentioned biogene removal is presented. Therefore, wastewater treated mechanically and biologically should be reported as wastewater treated biologically and the wastewater treated using all methods should be reported as treated using increased biogene removal method. Wastewater requiring treatment is wastewater which prior to discharge to the recipient, should undergo treatment processes.
